former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Nagai Nishi}}, {{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}, {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{jalink|Yao|Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{jalink|professional baseball player|Q11336312}} {{jalink|Keiji Obiki|Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{jalink|1618|Q6811}} ({{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} ({{jalink|Genbun|Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{jalink|Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{jalink|1729|Q6952}} to {{jalink|1735|Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{jalink|Goshu Naishi|Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{jalink|Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|Akagawa}}, {{jalink|Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{jalink|Torii|Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{jalink|Chukonhi monument|Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{jalink|Army General}} {{jalink|Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{jalink|Tamai Shrine}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Kamuwakatama-no-kami}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Nagai Nishi}}, {{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}, {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{jalink|Yao|Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{jalink|professional baseball player|Q11336312}} {{jalink|Keiji Obiki|Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{jalink|1618|Q6811}} ({{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} ({{jalink|Genbun|Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{jalink|Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{jalink|1729|Q6952}} to {{jalink|1735|Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{jalink|Goshu Naishi|Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{jalink|Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|Akagawa}}, {{jalink|Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{jalink|Torii|Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{jalink|Chukonhi monument|Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{jalink|Army General}} {{jalink|Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{jalink|Tamai Shrine}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Kamuwakatama-no-kami}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival == * {'text': '大阪市住吉区長居西2丁目1-4[1]', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '大阪府大阪市住吉区長居西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:神須牟地神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24874608}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kamisumuji Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kamisumuji Shrine.]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}{{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}{{jalink|Nagai Nishi}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Nagai Nishi}}, {{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}, {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{jalink|Yao|Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{jalink|professional baseball player|Q11336312}} {{jalink|Keiji Obiki|Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{jalink|1618|Q6811}} ({{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} ({{jalink|Genbun|Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{jalink|Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{jalink|1729|Q6952}} to {{jalink|1735|Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{jalink|Goshu Naishi|Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{jalink|Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|Akagawa}}, {{jalink|Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{jalink|Torii|Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{jalink|Chukonhi monument|Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{jalink|Army General}} {{jalink|Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{jalink|Tamai Shrine}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Kamuwakatama-no-kami}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{jalink|July 21|Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{jalink|October 21|Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}{{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}{{jalink|Nagai Nishi}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413}} and {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800|JR Hanwa Line}} {{jalink|Nagai Station|Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{jalink|Osaka City Bus|Q11441645}} {{jalink|Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{jalink|Nagai Park|Q5359309}} * {{jalink|Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street}} * {{jalink|Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441}} === Engineering Information Center |page=38-40 |date=2010-05-01 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪人|2010}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=Directory of Shrines in Osaka Prefecture |date=1971-01-22 |publisher=Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association |pages=76 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪府神道青年会|1971}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=History of Sumiyoshi Ward |date=1996-03-31 |publisher=70th Anniversary Project Committee, Sumiyoshi Ward |pages=537 |ref={{SfnRef|住吉区史|1996}}}} === Notes === === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === Sources === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|Nakatomi Sumuji Shrine}} * {{jalink|Sumuji == * {'text': '大阪市住吉区長居西2丁目1-4[1]', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '大阪府大阪市住吉区長居西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:神須牟地神社]] {{wikidata link|Q24874608}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kamisumuji Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kamisumuji Shrine.]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}{{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}{{jalink|Nagai Nishi}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Nagai Nishi}}, {{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}, {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{jalink|Yao|Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{jalink|professional baseball player|Q11336312}} {{jalink|Keiji Obiki|Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{jalink|1618|Q6811}} ({{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} ({{jalink|Genbun|Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{jalink|Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{jalink|1729|Q6952}} to {{jalink|1735|Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{jalink|Goshu Naishi|Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{jalink|Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|Akagawa}}, {{jalink|Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{jalink|Torii|Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{jalink|Chukonhi monument|Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{jalink|Army General}} {{jalink|Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{jalink|Tamai Shrine}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Kamuwakatama-no-kami}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{jalink|July 21|Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{jalink|October 21|Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}{{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}{{jalink|Nagai Nishi}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413}} and {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800|JR Hanwa Line}} {{jalink|Nagai Station|Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{jalink|Osaka City Bus|Q11441645}} {{jalink|Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{jalink|Nagai Park|Q5359309}} * {{jalink|Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street}} * {{jalink|Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441}} === References === * {{Cite journal|和書|title=Shrines and temples of Sumiyoshi: The shrine that listens well, Kamisumuji Shrine |journal={{jalink|Osakajin|Q11440031}} |publisher=Osaka City Urban Engineering Information Center |page=38-40 |date=2010-05-01 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪人|2010}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=Directory of Shrines in Osaka Prefecture |date=1971-01-22 |publisher=Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association |pages=76 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪府神道青年会|1971}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=History of Sumiyoshi Ward |date=1996-03-31 |publisher=70th Anniversary Project Committee, Sumiyoshi Ward |pages=537 |ref={{SfnRef|住吉区史|1996}}}} === Notes === === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === Sources === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|Nakatomi Sumuji Shrine}} * {{jalink|Sumuji Sone Shrine}} ===External links=== {{commonscat|Kamisumuji-jinja}} *[http://www.kamisumudi-jinjya.or.jp/ Engishiki-listed Kamisumuji Shrine, enshrined at Nagai] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{jalink|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Nagai Nishi}}, {{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}, {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former shrine rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{jalink|Yao|Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{jalink|professional baseball player|Q11336312}} {{jalink|Keiji Obiki|Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{jalink|1618|Q6811}} ({{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} ({{jalink|Genbun|Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{jalink|Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{jalink|1729|Q6952}} to {{jalink|1735|Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{jalink|Goshu Naishi|Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{jalink|1736|Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{jalink|Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|Akagawa}}, {{jalink|Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{jalink|Torii|Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{jalink|Chukonhi monument|Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{jalink|Army General}} {{jalink|Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{jalink|Tamai Shrine}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Kamuwakatama-no-kami}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{jalink|July 21|Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{jalink|October 21|Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{jalink|Osaka|Q35765}}{{jalink|Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702}}{{jalink|Nagai Nishi}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413}} and {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800|JR Hanwa Line}} {{jalink|Nagai Station|Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{jalink|Osaka City Bus|Q11441645}} {{jalink|Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{jalink|Nagai Park|Q5359309}} * {{jalink|Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street}} * {{jalink|Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441}} === References === * {{Cite journal|和書|title=Shrines and temples of Sumiyoshi: The shrine that listens well, Kamisumuji Shrine |journal={{jalink|Osakajin|Q11440031}} |publisher=Osaka City Urban Engineering Information Center |page=38-40 |date=2010-05-01 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪人|2010}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=Directory of Shrines in Osaka Prefecture |date=1971-01-22 |publisher=Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association |pages=76 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪府神道青年会|1971}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=History of Sumiyoshi Ward |date=1996-03-31 |publisher=70th Anniversary Project Committee, Sumiyoshi Ward |pages=537 |ref={{SfnRef|住吉区史|1996}}}} === Notes === === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === Sources === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === See also === * {{jalink|Nakatomi Sumuji Shrine}} * {{jalink|Sumuji Sone Shrine}} ===External links=== {{commonscat|Kamisumuji-jinja}} *[http://www.kamisumudi-jinjya.or.jp/ Engishiki-listed Kamisumuji Shrine, enshrined at Nagai] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill||lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}}
{{ill||lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}
{{ill||lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill||lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill||lt=Army General|UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill||lt=Tamai Shrine|UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill||lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill||lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413|WD=Q1192413}} and {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|lt=JR Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Nagai Station|Q1044114|WD=Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus|Q11441645|WD=Q11441645}} {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|WD=Q11440256|lt=Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Nagai Park|Q5359309|WD=Q5359309}} * {{ill||lt=Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441|WD=Q11440441}} === References === * {{Cite [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kamisumuji Shrine.]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Army General|UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamai Shrine|UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413|WD=Q1192413}} and {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|lt=JR Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Nagai Station|Q1044114|WD=Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus|Q11441645|WD=Q11441645}} {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|WD=Q11440256|lt=Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Nagai Park|Q5359309|WD=Q5359309}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441|WD=Q11440441}} === References === * {{Cite Association |pages=76 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪府神道青年会|1971}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=History of Sumiyoshi Ward |date=1996-03-31 |publisher=70th Anniversary Project Committee, Sumiyoshi Ward |pages=537 Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Army General|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kamisumuji Shrine.]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Army General|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413|WD=Q1192413}} and {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|lt=JR Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Nagai Station|Q1044114|WD=Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus|Q11441645|WD=Q11441645}} {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|WD=Q11440256|lt=Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Nagai Park|Q5359309|WD=Q5359309}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441|WD=Q11440441}} === former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Army General|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413|WD=Q1192413}} and {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|lt=JR Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Nagai Station|Q1044114|WD=Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus|Q11441645|WD=Q11441645}} {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|WD=Q11440256|lt=Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Nagai Park|Q5359309|WD=Q5359309}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441|WD=Q11440441}} === Urban Engineering Information Center |page=38-40 |date=2010-05-01 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪人|2010}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=Directory of Shrines in Osaka Prefecture |date=1971-01-22 |publisher=Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association |pages=76 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪府神道青年会|1971}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=History of Sumiyoshi Ward |date=1996-03-31 |publisher=70th Anniversary Project Committee, Sumiyoshi Ward Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|42.5|N|135|30|34.3|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with copper-sheet roof |別名 = Kamisumuji-sha |例祭 = 15th of each month (summer grand festival, autumn grand festival, monthly festival) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka city|label=Kamisumuji
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kamisumuji Shrine''' (かみすむぢじんじゃ[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/kyoiku/page/0000009080.html Osaka City designated cultural properties (FY2004)] ([https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/ Osaka City]).) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It is also called '''Kamisumuji-sha'''. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi Ōkami}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|Q849412|WD=Q849412|lt=Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === [[File:Kamisumuji-jinja history sign.JPG|thumb|250px|Origin board of the shrine.]] The shrine history board states: "An ancient Engishiki-listed shrine called Sannomiya, founded nearly 2,000 years ago." While {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}} was called the ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}}, this shrine was regarded as the sannomiya{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. In the Edo period, when daimyo worshiped at Sumiyoshi Taisha on sankin-kotai journeys, they were said to change from travel wear into prescribed formal attire at this shrine before worship{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39}}. Priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha often concurrently served here, but after WWII, amid postwar disorder, this shrine faced a crisis of survival. A priest from an old shrine in {{ill|lt=Yao|Q490872|WD=Q490872}} accepted this shrine's land without charge and became its first guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=39-40}}. The shrine's current origin text was researched and edited by his son, the second guji{{Sfn|Osakajin|2010|p=40}}The current guji is the son of the second guji (grandson of the first); his second son is former {{ill|lt=professional baseball player|Q11336312|WD=Q11336312}} {{ill|lt=Keiji Obiki|Q6383649|WD=Q6383649}}.. === Precinct === === Main shrine === Traditionally worshiped as a guardian deity of brewing and medicine, and known for strong divine virtue as a protector of both letters and arms. The main shrine buildings were destroyed by war in the Keicho era, then rebuilt on August 21, {{ill|lt=1618|Q6811|WD=Q6811}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} 4) by cooperation of four people including the Toyotomi retainer Tagaya{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. In the ninth month of {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} ({{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}} 1), the Tokugawa shogunate, concerned that this shrine and Tamai Shrine were declining, had two stone markers erected via mountain-merchant Ibei Yamaguchiya (Sugano Hirofusa){{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}. === Marker stone === This is one of marker stones set at 20 shrines that Edo-period Confucian scholar {{ill|lt=Seisho Namikawa|Q6563872|WD=Q6563872}} identified as previously-lost {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} shrines. Shikinaisha are shrines listed in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' (volumes 9 and 10), many of whose locations became unclear with the dissolution of the ancient state system. In the Edo period, momentum rose to clarify and honor these shrines. From {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952}} to {{ill|lt=1735|Q6984|WD=Q6984}}, Namikawa compiled ''{{ill|lt=Goshu Naishi|Q11372555|WD=Q11372555}}'', identifying all uncertain Shikinaisha in the province with existing shrines. Due to cost, full-scale commemoration was difficult, so marker stones were first erected for 20 shrines in Settsu. This was carried out from {{ill|lt=1736|Q6989|WD=Q6989}} into the following year with support of the Tokugawa shogunate, by Kubo Shigenobu under Namikawa's direction in Akagawa village (present {{ill|lt=Akagawa (Osaka)|Q76140132|WD=Q76140132|lt=Akagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Asahi-ku (Osaka)|Q861271|WD=Q861271|lt=Asahi Ward}}). All markers still exist, four within Osaka City. Before Namikawa's identification, this shrine was called by another name, "Sannomiya." Although parts of his textual criticism are now seen as insufficient, these markers are important materials showing development of early-modern empiricism and antiquarianism. === Gallery === Kamisumuji-jinja East Torii.JPG|{{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} (east side) Kamisumuji-jinja South Torii.JPG|Torii (south side) Kamisumuji-jinja Shamusho.JPG|Shrine office Kamisumuji-jinja Horse Statue.JPG|Dedicated horse statue A monument to the loyal dead in Kamisumuji-jinja.JPG|{{ill|lt=Chukonhi monument|Q11274518|WD=Q11274518}}. Calligraphy by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Army General|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Kantaro Inoue|Q11372988|WD=Q11372988}}. === Subsidiary shrines === === Outside-precinct branch shrines === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (deities: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Uganomitama-no-Mikoto}}|ウガノミタマノミコト}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamuwakatama-no-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}|カムワカタマノカミ}}, {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}}|ホグイノカミ}}) : An old Engishiki-listed shrine, also revered under the names "Nae-mi Shrine" and "Tanegashi Shrine." === Precinct branch shrines === * Agricultural Deity Shrine (deity: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto}}|オオナムチノミコト}}{{Sfn|Osaka Prefectural Shinto Youth Association|1971|p=76}}{{Sfn|History of Sumiyoshi Ward|1996|p=537}}) : Revered by farmers as a deity of cultivation. === Cultural properties === ; Osaka City designated cultural properties * Kamisumuji-sha name-marker stone * Tamai-sha name-marker stone === Festivals === * Summer Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=July 21|Q2727|WD=Q2727}} * Autumn Grand Festival - {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960}} * Monthly Festival - 21st of each month === Visitor information === ;Address * {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q35765|WD=Q35765}}{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku|Q985702|WD=Q985702}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Nishi|1=UNKNOWN}} 2-1-4 ;Access * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=Osaka Metro Midosuji Line|Q1192413|WD=Q1192413}} and {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|lt=JR Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Nagai Station|Q1044114|WD=Q1044114}}, about 7 minutes on foot (about 500 m west) * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus|Q11441645|WD=Q11441645}} {{ill|lt=Osaka City Bus Sumiyoshi Depot|Q11440256|WD=Q11440256|lt=Nagai Nishi-2 stop}}, immediately on foot ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Nagai Park|Q5359309|WD=Q5359309}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagai Ekimae Hondori Shopping Street|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Osaka Municipal Dairyo Junior High School|Q11440441|WD=Q11440441}} === Urban Engineering Information Center |page=38-40 |date=2010-05-01 |ref={{SfnRef|大阪人|2010}}}} * {{Cite |和書|title=Directory of Shrines in Osaka Prefecture |date=1971-01-22 |publisher=Osaka Prefectural
